questions.question: Why is autonomous research harder to close as a loop than AlphaEvolve-style algorithm search?
options: Research requires more GPUs
A research result has no machine-checkable correctness criterion — papers are judged by noisy peer review, not unit tests
Researchers refuse to share data
Code generation doesn't work for science
questions.explanation: AlphaEvolve's domains have tight evaluators; a paper does not. That makes the loop harder to close — and means the operational controls (sandbox, review, disclosure) carry most of the safety weight in AI Scientist v2.

questions.question: What did AI Scientist v2 achieve, and what is the honest framing of it?
options: It won a Nobel prize
One generated paper passed peer review at an ICLR 2025 workshop (with disclosure) — a proof of concept, not a reliability claim; workshop bars are lower and review is noisy
It replaced human reviewers at ICLR
It published independently in Nature with no human authors
questions.explanation: Both halves are true: the acceptance happened, and the independent evaluation found the system far from reliable. One acceptance under noisy review is a data point, not "the system does research."

questions.question: What did Beel et al.'s independent evaluation find?
options: The system was flawless but slow
42% of experiments failed from coding errors, the novelty check frequently mislabeled established concepts as novel, and polished figures masked experimental weaknesses
All generated papers were plagiarized
The system refused most research topics
questions.explanation: The third finding matters most: a system producing convincing outputs without convincing research is more dangerous than one that fails obviously. Evaluation must reach the underlying claims, not stop at the figure.

questions.question: Why does AI Scientist v2 demand stronger sandboxing than AlphaEvolve?
options: It uses more memory
It runs open-ended LLM-written code with open-ended goals — Sakana's own README warns of dangerous packages, uncontrolled web access, and unintended processes, recommending Docker at minimum
Its Python version is older
It must run on the public internet
questions.explanation: AlphaEvolve's tight evaluator bounds what executed code can be. v2's research loop doesn't have that bound, so isolation (Docker minimum, seccomp/gVisor preferred) plus manual review of anything leaving the system do the safety work.

questions.question: Comparing AlphaEvolve, DGM, and AI Scientist v2 — which has the weakest automatic evaluator, and what follows?
options: AlphaEvolve; it should be retired
AI Scientist v2 — peer review is weak and slow, the output surface is widest, and the path to public artifacts is shortest, so operational controls matter most there
DGM; SWE-bench is unreliable
All three have equally strong evaluators
questions.explanation: The phase's comparison table ranks them by evaluator rigor: unit tests + benchmarks (AlphaEvolve), benchmark suite (DGM), peer review (v2). As rigor drops and output surface widens, the burden shifts from evaluators to sandboxes and human gates.
